subsections: ROBERTSON'S SECONDARY THEOREM OF NATURAL SELECTION
metadata.source_title: Evolution and Selection of Quantitative Traits

blocks.content: In two separate papers, Robertson (1966a, 1968) suggested that the expected reponse to selection of any trait is a function of the covariance between the breeding value for that trait and relative fitness. Robertson (1968) called this relationship the secondary theorem of natural selection. While these two papers are usually jointly cited as the source for this theorem, a more careful reading shows that Robertson proposed two different formulae, based on whether the covariance with trait breeding value $ A_{z} $ involves the phenotype (w) or the breeding value $ (A_{w}) $ of relative fitness. As a result, there is confusion in the literature as to the exact form of Robertson's secondary theorem.
